title: Sound and consumer behaviour
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Consumer — music tempo, volume, and auditory cues in shopping environments.
learningObjectives: Explain how background music affects mood and pace.
Analyse tempo, volume, and genre effects on spending.
Evaluate Milliman's studies on music in retail.
Assess ethical issues of subconscious influence.

simpleExplanation.title: The soundtrack of shopping
simpleExplanation.summary: Slow music makes shoppers linger; fast music speeds them up; familiar tunes can boost mood and spending — all below conscious awareness.
steps: Slow tempo — longer dwell, more purchases.
Fast tempo — quicker turnover in restaurants.
Classical vs pop — perceived quality cues.

flashcards.back: Slower tempo → shoppers moved slower → significantly higher gross sales.
Fast music → faster eating and quicker table turnover; slow music → longer stays, more drinks ordered.
Loud/fast music increases physiological arousal — can boost energy purchases or cause avoidance if too intense.
Pleasant familiar music improves affect → more positive evaluation of environment and products.
Classical music in wine store → customers bought more expensive wine (North et al.) — quality cue via atmospherics.
Subconscious manipulation — shoppers unaware music tempo is altering behaviour (2.1.3 atmospherics debate).

sections.content: **Sound and consumers** (2.1.2) — cite **Milliman** and distinguish **arousal vs mood** mechanisms.

sections.question: A supermarket manager plays slow-tempo instrumental music at low volume during weekday afternoons. Average shopping time increases from 22 to 28 minutes and basket spend rises 8%. A fast-food chain plays upbeat pop at high volume at lunch. Table turnover increases but customer satisfaction scores fall. Explain and evaluate these findings using research on sound and consumer behaviour.
sections.solution:
**Supermarket — apply Milliman (1982):** **Slow tempo** reduces walking pace → longer **dwell time** → more items seen and purchased. Low volume maintains **comfortable arousal** without triggering avoidance (Mehrabian-Russell pleasure-arousal model, 2.1.3).

**Restaurant — apply Milliman (1986):** **Fast tempo** increases eating speed → faster **table turnover** (more covers per hour). High volume raises **arousal** — may speed behaviour but exceed optimal arousal for some diners, lowering **satisfaction**.

**Evaluate — strengths:** Both changes align with **replicated field studies** — high **ecological validity**, measurable behavioural outcomes (time, spend, turnover).

**Evaluate — limitations:** **Confounds** — weekday afternoon shoppers may differ demographically from other periods. **Individual differences** — age, culture, and music preference moderate effects. **Short-term only** — habituation may reduce impact over weeks. **Ethics** — subconscious influence without consent raises **manipulation** concerns; customers cannot make fully informed choices. **Reductionism** — music is one atmospheric variable among many (lighting, scent, layout — 2.1.1, 2.1.3).

sections.label: Mark a sound and consumer behaviour question
